entry.title: Re: Unable to change movement type in Freight Order Booki...
entry.content:
Good day @AndresCG 

Thank you for your question.

Your scenario points to two separate controls in Public Cloud TM:

  1. Incoterm and Incoterm Location 1 influences stage building in TM. Note that TM considers Incoterms during transportation planning for stage building and that planning and cost responsibility are transferred at Incoterm location 1.

See the following reference for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/61e246f4b34c4e1790d8b7651c0b40a8/87b11063db7348e384ebb5ed58c2769d.html?locale=en-US

  1. Movement Type on the ocean freight booking determines which stages are subcontracted in the booking. You can select the movement type in Manage Ocean Freight Bookings, for example Door to Door or Door to Port, and that the movement type determines the stages of a freight booking.

See the following reference for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/61e246f4b34c4e1790d8b7651c0b40a8/9386c8070e854c5cb4ceff469cd8c28e.html?locale=en-US

Because of the above-mentioned split, the answer to your main question is:

Question: Does Incoterm FOB automatically switch the booking to Door to Port?

Answer: No, Incoterm itself does not automatically change the freight booking movement type. What you should know is:

  • TM uses Incoterm location 1 for stage building.
  • In Manage Ocean Freight Bookings, you can select the movement type.

That means Incoterm helps define the freight unit stages and movement type is then selected on the booking and must be compatible with the assigned requirement documents.

So, entering FOB in the sales order by itself is not enough evidence that the booking will automatically switch to Door to Port.            

Regarding the /SCMTMS/OM171 error message and based on your screenshot, it highlights the point already mentioned above, which says once the freight booking already has assigned requirement documents or freight unit stages, you can no longer freely change the movement type because the movement type determines the booking stages. Changing it will conflict with the already assigned requirements.

Consult these references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/61e246f4b34c4e1790d8b7651c0b40a8/9386c8070e854c5cb4ceff469cd8c28e.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/588780cab2774a7ab9fffca3a7f919fe/1d38a387a64f479eafcf12fd38950b84.html?locale=en-US

So, the practical rule you should look into is to:

  • Set up the Incoterm data correctly before you create freight unit
  • Check the stage patter in your freight unit
  • Create the booking with the correct movement type from the start.
  • If the booking already has assigned required documents, you normally need to unassign the requirements or create a new booking rather than changing the movement type afterward.

 

Why you cannot choose the created ports in Inco. Location 1 on the sales order/tab?

Note that Incoterm locations are their own data. You can maintain them for relevant business objects and TM can use the Incoterm location and Incoterm versions from the assigned freight unit.

So, a location you created in Manage Locations is not automatically the same thing as a value that is immediately selectable in the sales order’s Inco. Location 1 field. In Public Cloud, this usually means you must also ensure that you maintain the location, so it is usable as an Incoterm location in the relevant master data and document.

See the following references for more insights

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/61e246f4b34c4e1790d8b7651c0b40a8/d5196924638042ffaf16e59344bff0c9.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/61e246f4b34c4e1790d8b7651c0b40a8/b21975bb49504065bd0afbcf11dbe569.html?locale=en-US

Regarding your question on configuration and check involved:

For your exact requirement, I would check the following:

  1. Sales order Incoterm data. Ensure the sales order contains correct Incoterm, correct Incoterm Location 1 and correct Incoterm version if you have some in your process.
  2. Check whether the port is maintained in a way that is valid for Incoterm location usage, not only as a generic TM location.
  3. After saving the sales order, review the created freight unit. If the freight unit does not already show the expected port related stage logic, the booking will not fix that root cause by itself.
  4. In Manage Ocean Freight Bookings, select the movement type that matches the freight unit stage pattern:
    1. Door to Port for export where pre carriage plus main carriage are intended
    2. Other movement types only if the requirement stages support them.
  5. If requirement documents are already assigned, do not try to switch the movement type on the existing booking. That error message in your screenshot shows it is blocked. Instead, correct the upstream data and replan.

 

To summarise with direct answers to your questions:

Question 1: I entered the Incoterm FOB in the sales order, expecting it to trigger the Door-to-Port movement type so that the stages would appear as pre-carriage and main carriage only. However, this did not happen

Answer: Because in Public Cloud TM, Incoterm data is used for stage building, while movement type is a booking field that is selected in the ocean freight booking. I am not aware of any documentation that states FOB alone automatically flips the booking movement type to Door to Port.

Question 2: Why can I not change the movement type afterward?

Answer: Because the booking already has requirement documents assigned to it, and the movement type determines the stages of the booking. This is most likely the reason for the error message.

Question 3: Why can I not select the ports created in Manage Location in Inco. Location 1?

Answer: Because Incoterm locations are handled separately from generic transportation locations. A port existing in Manage Locations does not by itself prove it is available an Incoterm Location 1 value in the sales document.

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: New POD 2.0 Framework: Interactive Value Journey (IVJ)
entry.content:
This blog post is part of a series on SAP Digital Manufacturing new POD 2.0 framework, the new generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing worker UIs.

New POD 2.0 Framework

  1. The New Generation of SAP Digital Manufacturing Worker UIs
  2. Template-Based Design Instead of POD Types
  3. Visual WYSIWYG Designer
  4. POD 2.0 Hands-on, Flexible Layout Capabilities, Part 1
  5. POD 2.0 Hands-on, Flexible Layout Capabilities, Part 2
  6. Custom UI Dialogs and Calling Production Processes
  7. Dynamic PODs, How to Dynamically Show, Hide, Enable, and Disable UI Elements
  8. Other Business Capabilities Enabled by POD 2.0
  9. Interactive Value Journey (IVJ)

🗓️ Timeline

POD 2.0 is currently in BETA with selected customers, General Availability (GA) is planned for 2605 release. BETA content is subject to change!

Interactive Value Journey

Discover how SAP Digital Manufacturing POD 2.0 (BETA) elevates the worker experience with dramatically faster load times, richer and more flexible user interfaces, and streamlined processes. POD 2.0 empowers citizen developers to design and extend Production Operator Dashboards using out-of-the-box low-code / no-code tools, enabling custom layouts and tailored capabilities, without heavy development effort.

Check: POD 2.0 Interactive Value Journey

Stay tuned for more! Thanks,
Manoel Costa

entry.title: Re: Business Process Model Connector (BPMC) Sync Solman 7...
entry.content:
Dear zabdain,

The Solman Connector (Business Process Model Connector) enables the import of initial folder structures, processes, and process step references/originals to Signavio. Attributes for processes and process step references/originals can also be mapped (see SAP help: https://help.sap.com/docs/signavio-process-transformation-suite/business-process-model-connector/map...). However, there are limitations regarding the types of fields supported for mapping. An upcoming update, expected in its initial version by the end of 2024 (see external Roadmap: https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?PRODUCT=F71E8E8587BC1EEE9FB5474ED9D6152D&q=business%2520process%2520m...), will allow synchronization of executables, with further enhancements anticipated in 2025 (not yet visible on public roadmap). As of now, this feature is not available. All other attachments, such as documents, test steps, etc., remain exclusively managed in SolMan and are not transferred to Signavio.

BR Nick

entry.title: Is there a good way to automatically export a printed SAC story with different input control filter?
entry.content:
Hi Experts,

I have a story that includes three different input control filters. I need to select various values in these filters and export the story as a PDF. However, the three filters result in a total of 90 combinations, which makes manual exporting impractical. I’m wondering whether there is any good way to automatically export a printed SAC story using different input control filter values.

I’ve considered using Publications with bookmarks, but 90 combinations would require creating 90 separate publications, and I don’t have enough licenses for that. I also looked into batch export, but it appears to support only a single story filter, so it doesn’t fit my use case.

Any tips or guidance would be highly appreciated! Thank you in advance!

SAP Analytics Cloud

entry.title: Understanding A2A and MCP: How Agents Communicate with Each Other vs the External World
entry.content:
The Agent Revolution: More Than Just Talking 

Over the past year, the concept of AI agents has evolved significantly. We are no longer limited to chat-based assistants that respond to prompts. Instead, organizations are building autonomous agents that can reason about a problem, plan next steps, and most importantly, take action within enterprise systems. This blog explains what it actually takes to make agents operational in real business environments, and why two complementary protocols - Model Context Protocol (MCP) and Agent-to-Agent Protocol (A2A) are essential for building reliable, scalable enterprise agent architectures.

While large language models provide the reasoning capability, the real enterprise value comes from enabling agents to interact with business systems. Updating a sales order in SAP S/4HANA, checking employee data in SuccessFactors, or triggering a workflow requires structured and secure communication with external systems. Acting in the real world is fundamentally different from generating text ,it requires clear interfaces, reliability, and governance.

As organizations design intelligent systems - whether building code-based agents on SAP BTP using frameworks such as LangGraph or AutoGen, or creating low-code/no-code agents in Joule Studio—they encounter two emerging standards that address different communication challenges:

  • Model Context Protocol (MCP)

  • Agent-to-Agent Protocol (A2A)

These standards are not competing alternatives. They solve different problems within an enterprise agent architecture. Understanding their distinct roles tool execution versus agent orchestration is key to building systems that are robust, maintainable, and scalable across the SAP landscape.

The Source of the Confusion: Two Solutions, Two Problems 

The confusion arose because the industry’s two leading challenges were addressed by two separate, but complementary, initiatives: 

  • Model Context Protocol (MCP): This standard was initially championed by Anthropic to solve the problem of Agent-to-Tool communication. It provides the standardized interface for an agent to reliably call external systems and services.
    • Further Reading: Anthropic’s Announcement [https://www.anthropic.com/news/model-context-protocol] 
  • Agent-to-Agent Protocol (A2A): This standard was announced by Google to solve the problem of Agent-to-Agent communication. It defines the universal language for specialist agents to discover, delegate tasks, and collaborate across different vendors and frameworks. 
    • Further Reading: Agent2Agent (A2A) Protocol, [https://a2a-protocol.org/latest/] 

Now, we will break down the two standards, examining the unique communication challenge each one solves: first, the Agent-to-Tool connection via MCP, and then, the Agent-to-Agent collaboration using A2A. 

MCP: The Agent's Standardized Handshake with Tools 

The Model Context Protocol (MCP) solves one of the most critical and messy challenges facing any agent developer: How do I ensure my LLM reliably and correctly calls the external APIs and services that provide its "hands and feet"? 

The Core Question: Why Can't the Agent Just Figure it Out? 

Many developers, especially those experienced with command lines (CLI) and web APIs, initially ask: "LLMs are smart enough to read API documentation. Why can't we just wire the LLM to an API and let it figure out the usage on its own 

This reveals the core weakness: The LLM is a brilliant thinker, but a messy doer when it comes to precise, repeatable system transactions." 

The Tool-Calling Problem Before MCP 

Before standardized protocols, tool calling was brittle and inefficient. An LLM had to be trained or prompted to correctly parse verbose documentation for every unique API. The problem wasn't a lack of connectivity; it was a lack of standardization in the instruction set given to the LLM.  

This led to three major issues: 

  1. Context Bloat: Developers had to feed the API's entire technical specification (JSON schema, parameter descriptions, error codes) into the LLM's prompt. This consumes valuable tokens and dramatically limits the complexity of the task the agent can handle, making it expensive and slow. 
  2. The Precision Gap (The Guessing Problem): With too much unstructured documentation, the LLM often had to guess the required input parameters or fabricated values, like guessing which buttons to press. This leads to hallucination in tool arguments, causing the function call to fail silently or crash the system, making the agent untrustworthy. 
  3. Security Risk: Giving an LLM raw, unstructured access to powerful tools increases the risk of the LLM misinterpreting the documentation and executing unauthorized actions. The interface was too open-ended, requiring massive overhead for safeguards. 

How MCP Solves It: Standardization and Reliability 

MCP solves this by moving the complexity of API translation out of the LLM's memory and into a standardized protocol layer. 

Simply put, MCP allows any LLM to talk to any tool without you having to write a custom, translation layer in between. 

  1. Standardized Interface: Instead of reading verbose documentation, the LLM receives a clean, canonical function declaration (e.g., fetch_tickets(team_id)). This structured input significantly improves the LLM's ability to decide what to use and how to invoke it. 
  2. Decoupling: The LLM only has to produce a simple, standardized output saying, "I want to call X function with Y arguments." The MCP framework then handles the messy work of translating that request into the specific, correct JSON or HTTP request for the backend tool. 

MCP in an SAP Context: The Universal Adapter 

Think of MCP as the Universal Adapter for your agent ecosystem. 

This standardization means your agent can use the exact same protocol to perform tasks across different Lines of Business (LoBs), such as: 

  • SuccessFactors: Submitting a vacation request. 
  • Ariba: Checking the status of a procurement contract. 
  • LeanIX: Querying your enterprise architecture landscape. 

While the agent uses the same protocol, each of these systems would typically be exposed via its own, distinct MCP server instance - for example, one server handling the HR tools, and a separate one handling the Finance tools. This allows for clear architectural separation, security segmentation, and better system governance. 

A2A: The Agent’s Language for Team Collaboration 

Now we move to the second piece of the puzzle: the Agent-to-Agent Protocol (A2A), which addresses the complex challenges of Agent-to-Agent communication. If MCP gives the agent its hands to act on a system, A2A gives the agent the ability to form a team and delegate work to other intelligent experts. 

The Orchestration Gap Before A2A 

Without a standard protocol, building complex systems was held back by two key failures in collaboration: 

  1. The Siloed Expertise Challenge (Framework Lock-in): Different development teams used different agent frameworks. An agent built in one could not easily speak to an agent built in another. The result was siloed expertise -  your Financial Agent could not talk to your Logistics Agent if they weren't built on the exact same technology stack. 
  2. Unstable Coordination (The "Fire and Forget" Delegation): Complex enterprise tasks require delegation, but before A2A, hand-offs were custom-coded and unstable. When an agent asked another agent for help, it had to know the specific, non-standard API for that request. If the receiving agent failed, crashed, or took too long, the calling agent often had no robust, standardized way to track the task, check its status, or formalize a failure report. It was a one-way, "fire and forget" communication system. 

Why Delegation Requires a Protocol 

Complex enterprise tasks are too large for a single agent. A human orchestrator doesn't just send a quick, static request to a colleague; they define the scope, set the status, establish a task ID, and expect a formal status report. A2A formalizes this exact multi-step process for agents. 

A2A defines the universal language for agents to: 

  • Discover other available expert agents (using the AgentCard). 
  • Define a Task (specifying inputs, outputs, and requirements). 
  • Delegate that Task across different frameworks and vendors. 
  • Track the Task Lifecycle (from pending to completed, allowing for retries and error handling). 

 

The Critical Distinction: Why Not Just Use MCP to Call Another Agent? 

This is the most common and important question that architects and developers ask. You think: If MCP is so good at calling a tool, why can't we just define another agent as a tool and call it via MCP? 

While you could technically structure a basic interaction this way, it completely misses the complex requirements of true agent collaboration and the fundamental difference between a static service and an intelligent peer:

  1. Stateless Interaction vs. Stateful Collaboration(The Context Problem):
    • MCP (Tool): When an agent calls a tool via MCP, the interaction follows a stateless request–response pattern. The agent sends a function call (for example, check_inventory(product_id)), the tool executes the operation, and returns the result.
    • A2A (Agent): In contrast, when agents communicate through A2A, the receiving agent can maintain task state, memory, and context across multiple interactions. An agent can reason over previous messages, track the lifecycle of a delegated task, and coordinate multi-step processes before returning a result.
  2. MCP is for Execution, A2A is for Delegation: MCP is built for a simple, single-input, single-output function call that executes immediately (e.g., "Get the current time"). A2A is built for a complex, multi-step conversation and delegation (e.g., "Please handle this entire process, check back with me if you need clarification, and report success when done"). 
  3. Discovery vs. Static Call: MCP requires the calling agent to know the exact specific endpoint and function signature (it's a static, one-to-one call). A2A provides the Agent Card specification, enabling dynamic discovery - the agent can ask: "Who is the best Finance Agent available right now to handle this?" 

The answer is clear- MCP is for reliable action execution (connecting brains to hands), while A2A is for reliable workflow delegation (connecting one brain to another brain). For true enterprise workflows, you need the task management, memory, and discovery features that only A2A provides. 

Where is SAP? The Roadmap for MCP and A2A 

Enterprise standards only become powerful when they are natively supported by the platforms we use. Here is how SAP is currently bridging the gap between AI agents and business data: 

  1. MCP in Joule Studio (Available Today) 

This is the developer-facing capability. If you are building a custom agent in Joule Studio, MCP is already integrated and ready for use. 

  • Direct Connectivity: Joule Studio allows your agents to connect to any external MCP server. 
  • Tool Discovery: Once a connection is established, the agent can dynamically pull in and use the specific tools provided by that server—whether they are for querying databases, interacting with third-party web services, or accessing SAP-specific utilities. 

The MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite (API Management) 

The MCP Gateway (Upcoming) is the Enterprise Architect's control plane. It solves the challenge of "MCP sprawl" by providing a single entry point for all agentic tools. 

  • Centralized Hub: It acts as a single gateway that takes your vast catalog of existing SAP APIs and Integration Flows and exposes them as hosted MCP servers. 
  • Governance: It allows the IT organization to manage, secure, and govern which tools are available to AI agents across the entire company, rather than managing individual agent-to-tool connections. 

Technical Reference: Model Context Protocol in SAP API Management 

2. The A2A Roadmap (Agent-to-Agent)

InitiativeDescriptionRole of A2ATarget Delivery
System-Triggered AgentsExternal 3rd-party systems triggering Joule agents.Trigger Point: External systems trigger Joule agents via A2A protocol.On the Roadmap
Bring Your Own Agent (BYOAJoule orchestrating external 3rd-party agents (e.g., LangChain).Action Type: Joule sends an A2A message/send to the agent.

Available

 

Conclusion: Building the Unified Agent Ecosystem 

While MCP and A2A have arrived at a similar time, they are not competing standards. Instead, they represent the two essential layers required to build autonomous, enterprise-grade AI systems.

Your modern, robust agent architecture must use both: A2A handles the high-level orchestration and delegation of tasks across your team of specialist agents, while MCP handles the low-level execution of specific commands against your tools and APIs. 

By adopting both of these open standards, you move beyond simple single-agent chatbots and begin architecting true, scalable, and resilient enterprise automation on any modern cloud platform hosting your line-of-business applications. 

References & Further Reading

If you are ready to move from theory to implementation, these resources provide the technical blueprints for building with MCP and A2A today:

  • From Prompt to Production: Creating an MCP Server for Joule Studio – A hands-on guide to building and deploying a custom MCP server on SAP BTP using AI-generated code to give Joule new "hands."

  • Technical Tutorial: Connecting LangGraph Agents via A2A – The "External" part: How to build your own custom agent in Python and make it A2A-compliant.

  • Code-Based Agents Developer Guide – The formal documentation for A2A schemas, protocol versioning, and the agent-request action.

  • MCP in SAP API Management – Documentation for the MCP Gateway in SAP Integration Suite to manage and govern agentic tools at scale.

entry.title: Error subscribing SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition and Cloud Identity Services in trial subacc.
entry.content:
Hello,

I am currently trying to follow the course "Develop extensions with CAP following the SAP BTP Developer's Guide". 

When following the guide I have to add the service plan "SAP Build Work Zone, standard edition free (Application)" and I have to subscribe to it via Services → Service Marketplace. 

However the creation fails and returns the following error

"You haven't configured your subaccount (for business users) to trust a tenant of SAP Cloud Identity Services using OpenID Connect. SAML trust isn't supported for this service. To subscribe this application, use the Establish Trust option to configure trust between your subaccount and SAP Cloud Identity Services.
For more information, see https://help.sap.com/docs/btp/sap-business-technology-platform/establish-trust-and-federation-between-uaa-and-identity-authentication."

I have created a standard SAP account and I am logged in via the Default identity provider

I also don't have any other tenants to configure.

To resolve this issue I then tried to also subscribe to Cloud Identity Services. This unfortunately also doesn't work and returns the following error message "Cloud Identity Services konnte nicht abonniert werden. Bitte versuchen Sie es erneut. Falls das Problem weiterhin besteht, stellen Sie eine Frage in der SAP Community."

Now I am stuck and can't further proceed with the course. I have also tried it with a completely different SAP account and trial subaccount. The same issues arise.

Is this issue already known and is there any way to resolve this?

Kind regards

entry.title: SuccessFactors Recruiting - Docusign offer letters
entry.content:
Hi,

We have integrated docusign successfully in the production instance, below are the steps followed for the integration, but still when recruiter tries to send esignature offer letter there is an error popup displayed.

Step 1: Enable SuccessFactors Esignature in the provisioning.
Step 2: Under recruiting settings Enabled electronic signature for offer letters --> Third-Party eSignature Environment --> Production. Then provided the Docusign content grant access and entered the admin credentials and allowed access.
Step 3: Entered DocuSign User ID and DocuSign API Account ID --> Save.
Step 4: Recruiter initiates online offer with esignature and the recruiter logged into docusign and provided access to the system, recruiter now tries select Online offer with esignature got the following error

I have reviewed all the KBA relating to subject line character limit and checked if same account is connected with  multiple instance, additionally I have also checked the Partner_username in manage external user accounts and docusign user id is same.

Can someone help me with this issue?

Thanks
Manovishwas

entry.title: Re: How to enable auto -reversing function for the manual...
entry.content:
Hello @Cheng_Huang58 

Thank you for your question.

Yes, your requirement is addressed in the Public Cloud. The feature is Posting Journal Entries with Auto Reverse. When you post the entry, you should enter a Planned Reversal Date and Reversal Reason, and the system can automatically reverse the journal entry in the following month through a scheduled general ledger job.

This is the recommended solution when you currently revers accruals or month end adjustment entries manually or one by one. You may also use Manage Journal Entry Templates, Upload General Journal Entries, Manage Journal Entries, and Schedule General Ledger Jobs.

Here a quick guide to the recommended steps in your system:

  • Post the journal entry and fill your planned reversal date and reason.

  • Check the posted document in Manage Journal Entries to confirm the reversal data is stored.

  • Schedule the Post General Journal Entries with Auto Reverse job in Schedule General Ledger Jobs.

  • If you uploaded postings from a spreadsheet, include STODT and STGRD in the upload file. See 3591371 - Can't upload auto-reverse postings in Upload General Journal Entries app - SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition - SAP for Me for more details.
  • If the process is more than a simple reversal and requires systematic accrual calculation, evaluate Accruals Management and its Deferral of expense and revenues instead.

Consult these references for more details

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/38cad17bfd644c8c8c8b29f9e010c85f.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/950f8054747b053de10000000a441470.html?locale=en-US

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0fa84c9d9c634132b7c4abb9ffdd8f06/a68062a6cd8a46d8aea1fcfd3b620d0b.html?locale=en-US

Let me know if you have further questions.

Best regards

Chris

entry.title: Re: SAP BUILD PROCESS AUTOMATION - Project not loading
entry.content: Hi, This issue is often related to the browser being used. SBPA usually works best with Google Chrome or Microsoft Edge . If the project page is not loading and shows a blank screen, try the following: Open the SAP Build lobby in Incognito mode Try accessing it with another browser such as Microsoft Edge or Google Chrome From recent experience, Microsoft Edge seems to work more reliably, while Firefox or Safari sometimes have loading issues with SAP Build.

entry.title: Re: Manager tagging in the system
entry.content:
Hi Gelo,

In SAP SuccessFactors, the system typically identifies a user as a manager based on their organizational relationships, specifically whether they have direct reports.

  • The system usually identifies a user as a manager if they have direct reports assigned to them in the organizational hierarchy. This is a standard behavior because the role of a manager is inherently tied to having subordinates.
  • Ensure that the direct reports are correctly assigned in the system.
  • There might be a configuration issue where the manager relationship is not properly set up.
  • There could be data synchronization issues where the changes are not reflected immediately.

For detailed guidance, please refer to the SAP SuccessFactors documentation:

  • SAP SuccessFactors Employee Central Documentation
  • Checking Manager Permission | SAP Help Portal

I hope I could help! 🙂

Best Regards,

Tündi
Product Support - People | Platform
SAP Support

entry.title: Re: 999 – The Number of the BSEG
entry.content:
Hello Jack,

I very much fear I cannot help you here...since I am not really a specialist for these DRC reports or even tax payables postings. So what happens if RFUMSV00 tries to generate a batch-input session with more than 999 items? The typical error message such as F5 727 or F5 734? I would have hoped it already includes a mechanism to just start a new document whenever the 999 limit is reached. What I can tell you for sure is that no BSEG summarization will be performed in this case (and there is also no option for such a summarization). As already mentioned several times, traditional FB01-like postings are really based on BSEG, they build up BSEG items and later post them to ACDOCA. So we can't summarize them in a later step, the BSEG items are the source / the trigger of the whole transaction in this case ("prima nota"). The only technical option in this case is really to split the documents...or in your case (if the RFUMSV00 batch-input session doesn't take care of this), to manually post the tax payables postings in several smaller batches.

I would maybe ask the question in a ticket (to a component such as FI-GL-GL-F), how to find a temporary solution based on RFUMSV00 until the gap is fixed in the DRC statutory report.

Sorry for no better answer,

Kind regards, 

Stefan

entry.title: Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management in SAP S/4HANA Cloud, Private Edition 2025 FPS1
entry.content:
Here’s an overview of the Enterprise Portfolio and Project Management area, highlighting the latest enhancements across Project System, Portfolio and Project Management, and Commercial Project Management. For additional details, you can explore the What's New Viewer. To see what’s planned next, take a look at the EPPM roadmap.

Project System

Report to display and delete object-based authorizations

Object-based authorizations have been initially introduced with SAP S/4HANA 2025. In FPS01 we provided a new administration report to display or delete object-based authorization records for projects or networks. You can either delete object-based authorizations completely or, alternatively, enable admin authorizations to be retained. For documentation purposes it is also possible to export an overview of the object-based authorizations in various formats prior to the deletion.

Report for displaying and deleting object-based authorizations

 

Visualize projects and WBS elements on a map

 

By leveraging SAP Geographical Enablement Framework (GEF) for the Project System, you can now assign geo-spatial data to your projects and WBS elements. Using the Spatial Data Editor shown below, you select a project or WBS element and then assign geo-spatial data to it.

Adding geo-spatial information for a project

 

Once the geo-spatial data is maintained, you can use the Spatial Data Explorer app, to analyze data of project system objects in the context of geographical locations.

Viewing project in Spatial Data Explorer app

 In the example delivery, the projects and WBS elements are groups according to status. By clicking on a project or WBS element in the map, additional attributes of the business object are displayed. These attributes are configurable. You can also forward navigate e.g. to Project Builder to view more details.

By showing the projects and WBS elements on the map, it becomes e.g. easier to analyze dependencies between project activities and maintenance activities based on spatial proximity.

 

Store Planned Project Revenues from Sales Documents or WBS Billing Plans in ACDOCP table

Planned revenues from sales quotation items or sales document items account assigned to WBS elements are now saved in the central financial planning table ACDOCP.  The same holds true for planned revenues planned from WBS billing plans.   With that it is possible to use this information in the respective reporting apps, such as Project Cost Overview and Project Cost Line Items and can be also copied into reporting categories. More details can be found on help.sap.com 

Project Cost Overview with planned revenue from sales order

 

 

Support of network account-assigned purchase requisitions and orders in Manage Project Procurement app

The Manage Project Procurement app (F2930) was enhanced to now also display  purchase requisitions and purchase orders that are account assigned to networks or network activities. To enable effective filtering the app filters have also been extended accordingly to enable filtering on networks and network activities.

Network Activity assigned Purchasing Documents

 

 

Default Value for Purchasing Organization Derived Based on User Parameter (3633411)

For external procurement in a network, users have to maintain a purchasing organization on the detail tab of material components. With this enhancement the user parameter for purchasing organization is considered for material components.

Portfolio and Project Management

Additional OData APIs and Data Product for Portfolio Management

Portfolio Management OData API has been enhanced by the following capabilities:

  • Create/Update/Delete for Portfolio Initiative Financial and Capacity Plan data
  • Read Responsible and Role for Portfolio Initiative
    The role and their staffings for a portfolio initiative, as well as the responsible role and responsible resource for portfolio initiative and their decision points can be retrieved via Read API.
  • Enhanced assigned Project creation for Portfolio Item
    The create project action for portfolio items has been enhanced by additional parameters that allow consumers to define which template and object link type shall be leveraged for the creation of the project. So far, only the default template was considered.
  • Create Portfolio Item from template
    By passing the item template UUID, bucket UUID and (optional) a description, a new portfolio item is created. To alter the created portfolio item, the portfolio item change API can be used.

Continuous Improvements based on Continuous Influence Session (3593568)

Enhanced Report to Import Portfolio Items from External File

The utility report RPM_DX_ITEM to create or update portfolio items via Excel Upload has been enhanced as follows:

To create assigned projects for the portfolio item via the report, the fields PS_LOGSYSTEM, CPRO_TEMPLATE and CREATE_CPROJECT can be used. The field PS_LOGSYSTEM is no longer mandatory, if left empty the system maintained in the standard object link for Portfolio Item – Project System integration (0INMPSPD) is used. The field CPRO_TEMPLATE which is used to specify which project (for PPM or PS project) shall be used as a template now accepts both the internal and the external ID of the PS Project.

The report now also allows for subsequent creation of a CPM project in case e.g. the PS project already exists and the customizing of the item type is setup for CPM and PS project creation. This can be achieved by running the report in the change mode and setting the flag CREATE_CPROJECT to ‘X’.

Enable Sorting of View/Category/Group in Financial and Capacity Planning

You can now define the sequence of the views, categories and groups in the financial and capacity planning user interface via a sort number in the customizing. The sort number can be defined globally on the view (see example below), category and group level. It is also possible to overwrite these global settings for a specific portfolio type.

CustCustomizing for Financial Views with new column "Sequence"

 

  

 

Commercial Project Management

Copy commercial Project

With this feature, you can quickly create a new commercial project by duplicating an existing project. The functionality allows you to selectively copy key project elements, including the financial plan, planning data, risks and opportunities, issues and change requests, as well as the team structure and project members

Copying of commercial projects can be accessed via a separate Fiori tile.

 

Change documents for risks and opportunities

Change documents enable tracking of the changes to risk and opportunity records, so that the project managers can track what was changed, when, and by whom, for better auditability and accountability.Change documents for risk management

 

 

 

Enhancements to Quick Financial Planning capabilities are described in detail in this blog

entry.title: Re: Transformation Fatigue: The Hidden Risk in Continuous Business Change
entry.content:
The Transaction Beneath the Fatigue: A Four-Laws Reading

First, I need to say thank you for very much this piece.

It names something very real that many of us recognise but few internal publications address with this level of honesty — the quiet, cumulative exhaustion of people who have been "in transformation" for so long that the word has lost its meaning. The PACE framework is a remarkably useful contribution.

I want to offer a complementary lens. Not to replace the psychological framing — the fatigue is real and the human cost matters, a lot — but to suggest that what we are seeing as fatigue may be the symptoms of something more structural. If we can identify the underlying dynamics, we may be able to intervene earlier and more precisely.

And the data suggests that intervening earlier is not optional. The OP cites McKinsey's finding that 70% of transformations fail. That figure is from 1995 — a different world. By 2024, Bain's updated research puts the general failure rate at 88%. For digital transformations specifically, Bain and McKinsey data shows failure rates approaching 95%. Meanwhile, their research indicates that the average corporate employee has survived multiple failed transformation projects and is currently enduring up to 10 per year.

To not expect a degree of stress in such circumstances is to not be human.

Four Dynamics of Sustainable Exchange

There is a body of organisational and economic research suggesting that all productive human exchange — commercial, collaborative, employment-based — depends on four conditions being continuously maintained. When they are met, exchange creates value (i.e. mutual profit) and can be sustained indefinitely. When they are violated, the exchange degrades in predictable ways and so too does profit. These aren't rules to be imposed but dynamics that are always operating, whether we attend to them or not.

I'll describe each briefly, note the research tradition it draws from, and then connect it to the specific patterns the original post identifies.

1. Boundaries Are Respected and Only Crossed With Consent

Every working relationship has boundaries — of role, of workload, of personal information, of how and when and under what conditions a person's professional life can be altered. Productive exchange requires that these boundaries are only crossed with the genuine participation of the person affected.

This is well-established across multiple research traditions. Rousseau's work on psychological contracts (1989, 1995) demonstrated that employees operate under implicit agreements with their organisation that extend far beyond formal terms, and that unilateral violation of these contracts produces cynicism, disengagement, and withdrawal of discretionary effort — even when the change itself might be reasonable. Deci and Ryan's Self-Determination Theory identifies autonomy as a basic psychological need; decades of research confirm that when people's domain of action is overridden without their participation, intrinsic motivation collapses (Deci & Ryan, 2000). The procedural justice literature (Thibaut & Walker, 1975; Colquitt, 2001) consistently shows that having genuine voice in decisions that affect you is one of the strongest predictors of acceptance — even when the outcome is unfavourable.

The key word here (as you noted) is genuine. Consultation theatre — where input is solicited but the decision is already made — is worse than no consultation, because it performs consent while denying it.

2. Value Is Exchanged Within Satisfactory Volumes and Horizons

Trade is not an exchange of equal values — transaction costs alone make that impossible. Trade occurs because the parties hold different valuations of what is exchanged, and it is this very asymmetry that creates value. Both parties profit, or the exchange doesn't happen. This is foundational in the subjective value tradition in economics (Menger, 1871; Mises, 1949) and explains why a business — a formalised network of internal transactions — multiplies value across its structure in ways no individual could achieve alone.

But the exchange must remain satisfactory to both parties, in both volume (is the ratio of contribution to return acceptable?) and horizon (are promises of future value credible given past delivery?). Adams' Equity Theory (1963) established that people continuously evaluate their input-to-outcome ratio and adjust behaviour when it feels unbalanced. The behavioural economics literature on temporal discounting shows that willingness to accept present costs for future benefits depends critically on trust and track record. Eisenberger's Perceived Organisational Support research (1986, 2001) demonstrates that employees form a global assessment of whether the organisation values their contribution — and that this assessment predicts commitment, performance, and retention with remarkable consistency.

Both dimensions — volume and horizon — must be satisfied simultaneously. An exchange that is fair today but based on promises that never materialise is not a viable transaction. The connection — the employment relationship — that produces the surplus value, will break.

3. Context Is Shared Sufficient to Allow Required Performance

People cannot perform well in conditions they cannot understand. When one party to a transaction withholds, distorts, or fails to provide the context the other party needs, performance degrades — and the context-deprived party is typically blamed for the failure.

The economics of information asymmetry (Akerlof, 1970; Spence, 1973; Stiglitz, 2000 — all Nobel-recognised work) establishes that asymmetric information between transacting parties produces market failures. Weick's sensemaking theory (1995) demonstrates that people in organisations must construct a coherent narrative of what is happening and why in order to act effectively — and that when the environment becomes too complex or contradictory for sensemaking to succeed, performance collapses. The shared mental models literature (Cannon-Bowers & Salas, 2001) confirms that team performance depends on common understanding of task, environment, and roles.

Context-sharing is not a leadership style preference. It is a structural requirement of any transaction where performance is expected.

4. Voice and Exit Are Viable Without Penalty

When conditions in an exchange become unsatisfactory, the parties need mechanisms to correct them. There are two such mechanisms: voice (articulating what is wrong so it can be addressed) and exit (leaving the exchange). Both must be genuinely available, without penalty, at all times.

This builds on Hirschman's foundational Exit, Voice, and Loyalty framework (1970), but with an important extension. Hirschman treated exit and voice as alternatives. In practice, both must be viable simultaneously. An organisation where exit is easy but voice is punished will lose its best people without ever learning why. An organisation where voice is encouraged but exit is penalised (through cultural stigma, golden handcuffs, or the subtle reputational costs of leaving mid-transformation) accumulates cynicism, because voice without the credible backdrop of exit lacks force.

Edmondson's psychological safety research (1999, 2019) provides extensive evidence that teams where voice is safe outperform those where it isn't — a finding confirmed at scale by Google's Project Aristotle. The turnover literature consistently shows that the highest performers, who have the most options, exit first when conditions deteriorate (March & Simon, 1958; Hom & Griffeth, 1995). The whistleblower retaliation literature documents the chilling effect when voice is penalised, and the escalating dysfunction that follows when correction mechanisms are disabled.

The Scale of the Problem

Before connecting the dynamics to the symptoms, it is worth pausing on the data, because the data is sobering.

Prosci's Best Practices in Change Management research (12th Edition) tracks organisational change saturation over time. The proportion of organisations operating beyond their change capacity rose from 59% in 2007 to 73% in 2019 — and that was before the pandemic accelerated digital transformation timelines across every industry. When saturation is reached, the consequences are measurable and consistent: 54% of employees seek new roles, 48% report increased stress, and 37% admit they trust their employers less.

The research consensus among industry analysts is that employees can sustainably absorb one to two major changes per year. Yet over 50% of business leaders plan three or more *concurrent* changes in the next two years. The gap between what is planned and what is humanly sustainable is not a rounding error. It is structural overcommitment.

Gartner's research (2023/24) and Harvard Business Review reporting show the trajectory in starker terms. Employee willingness to support enterprise change dropped from 74% in 2016 — already dangerously low — to 43% in 2022, a collapse of over 40%. During the same period, the number of planned enterprise changes per year rose from two in 2016 (already at the consensus capacity limit) to ten in 2022 — a fivefold increase against a workforce whose willingness to engage was simultaneously halving.

The human cost is precise: when change fatigue takes hold, intent to stay declines by 42% while performance declines by 27% (Gartner, 2023). Note the disparity. Performance drops less than commitment. People whose intent to stay has collapsed are still trying to make an impossible situation work. That is not disengagement. That is professionalism operating on fumes.

And 82% of HR leaders agree that their managers are not equipped to lead concurrent changes. I would reframe "equipped": they are not resourced. The capability may exist. The bandwidth, authority, and organisational support to exercise it across simultaneous initiatives does not.

Connecting the Dynamics to the Symptoms

With this framework and this data in mind, revisit the patterns the original post identifies. What becomes visible is a cause-and-effect structure beneath the fatigue.

Initiative Stacking → Boundary Violation (Dynamic 1) + Context Fragmentation (Dynamic 3)

When employees are absorbing change across multiple simultaneous transformation programs, each with its own governance and demands, the terms under which they agreed to work have been fundamentally altered — without renegotiation. Their boundaries of role, workload, and operating context are being crossed repeatedly and from multiple directions at once.

Simultaneously, they face not just cognitive overload but context incoherence. Each initiative has its own narrative, its own metrics, its own vocabulary. When the ERP transformation requires standardised processes while the digital initiative demands agile experimentation while the restructuring eliminates the teams doing both — the shared context required for performance doesn't just become insufficient. It becomes contradictory. No amount of town halls resolves a context that is this broken at the structural level. No amount of employee loyalty or professionalism can make the impossible work.

The original post describes employees who "maintain business as usual while absorbing change across multiple dimensions." The four-dynamics lens suggests this isn't a bandwidth problem — it's an impossibility dressed up as an expectation. With the research consensus at one to two sustainable changes per year and employees currently enduring 2 to 5 times that level, the gap is not something that better communication or pacing can bridge. The arithmetic simply doesn't work.

The Promise-Reality Gap → Value Exchange Default (Dynamic 2)

When a major rollout is described as "empowering people with better tools" but delivers six months of degraded productivity, increased workarounds, and remedial training — the organisation has defaulted on its side of a transaction. Employees traded their accumulated mastery, their stable processes, their competence and identity for a promise of improved capability. They gave up something real. They received something worse, on a timeline they didn't agree to.

The "sophisticated internal algorithm" that employees develop — discount the promise, brace for disruption, wait 18 months — is not cynicism. It is rational pricing of risk by a counterparty that has been burned repeatedly. By the third or fourth transformation, the organisation is asking employees to extend credit — emotional, cognitive, behavioural credit — against a track record of non-delivery. With failure rates at 88% generally and 95% for digital transformations, the employees' discount rate is, if anything, too generous. That the credit is no longer extended is not a failure of employee engagement. It is the predictable consequence of repeated default. This is exactly how every company treats a non-paying customer.

The original post observes that "the credibility of future transformation promises is eroded." The four-dynamics lens reframes this: it's not a credibility problem. It's a solvency problem. The organisation's promises have been devalued by its own history. And the Gartner data confirms the consequence: willingness to support change has collapsed from 74% to 43% in six years. The market has priced in the risk.

Loss of Agency → Boundary Violation (Dynamic 1) + Failed Voice (Dynamic 4)

The original post notes that "sustained transformation without genuine participation destroys the sense of agency that motivates high performance." This is Dynamics 1 and 4 operating in concert. Boundaries are crossed without consent (Dynamic 1), and when employees attempt to voice their experience, they encounter consultation theatre rather than genuine influence (Dynamic 4).

The OP identifies middle management as particularly affected — "asked to implement change they had no hand in designing, while managing upward anxiety and downward morale simultaneously." This is a precise description of people trapped in a transaction where their boundaries are being overridden from above, their voice is filtered before it reaches decision-makers, and their exit is complicated by seniority, compensation structures, and professional identity. All four dynamics are in violation simultaneously, which is why middle management burnout in transformation contexts is so acute and so predictable. The 82% of HR leaders who agree their managers aren't equipped to lead concurrent changes are observing this dynamic from the outside. The managers themselves are living it.

Unacknowledged Grief → Value Destruction (Dynamic 2) + Context Withholding (Dynamic 3)

The OP observation that "every transformation involves loss" is exactly right, and the four-dynamics lens reveals why this matters beyond the emotional dimension. When familiar processes, team structures, and career pathways are dismantled, employees lose accumulated capital — not just emotional attachment, but real professional value built over years. Mastery of the old system was an asset. Social capital within the old team structure was an asset. Understanding of how things actually worked (as opposed to how the org chart said they worked) was an asset.

When leadership "focuses entirely on the destination and fails to acknowledge what is being left behind," it is withholding context (Dynamic 3) — specifically, the context that would make the loss legible as a real cost rather than mere sentimentality. And it is refusing to account for the value destroyed (Dynamic 2) — treating employees' accumulated investment as worthless because the organisation has decided to move on.

The shadow spreadsheets that proliferate after ERP implementations are not resistance. They are capital preservation — employees protecting the only asset the transformation didn't destroy. And here is what the four-dynamics lens makes visible: these shadow systems, these workarounds, these unofficial processes that technically violate the new way of working — they are often what actually keeps the business running. The knowledge of "how to actually get things done" in the context of unfinished system upgrades, incomplete transformation changes, and multiple previously failed initiatives, is literally priceless. It exists almost exclusively among the people closest to the work. Very few of the decision-makers who designed the transformations possess it.

The organisational instinct to eliminate shadow systems rather than capture the intelligence they represent — to treat them as non-compliance rather than as a signal about what the new system fails to provide — is a Dynamic 3 failure compounding a Dynamic 2 failure. The employees are showing you, through their behaviour, exactly where the new system doesn't meet operational reality. That information is extraordinarily valuable. Punishing it is, to put it gently, suboptimal.

Quiet Disengagement → Failed Voice and Exit (Dynamic 4)

The OP opens with what is perhaps its most important observation: fatigue "manifests not as open rebellion but as quiet disengagement — the meeting attendance that becomes performative, the survey scores that plateau, the tribal knowledge that quietly walks out the door."

This is the signature of Dynamic 4 failure. Voice has been tested and found non-viable — speaking honestly about transformation problems carries risk (being labelled resistant, negative, not a team player) while producing no correspondingly beneficial change, i.e. it produces no profit for the employee who speaks. Exit has not yet been exercised, but the conditions for it are accumulating. The "tribal knowledge that quietly walks out the door" is exit in slow motion — not dramatic resignation, but the gradual withdrawal of discretionary contribution by people who are mentally cataloguing their options. The Prosci data confirms what happens next: 54% seek new roles. Intent to stay drops by 42%.

Emily Moore's research at the University of Toronto on engineering and systems thinking offers a critical insight here: "when one person is vocally resisting, there are probably 100 to 200 others thinking the same thing who have not spoken up." This maps to what we know about the distribution of agreeableness in personality research — roughly 1% of people are sufficiently committed to truth-telling that they will voice dissent even at personal cost to themselves. The other 99% are not agreeing. They are silent, and silence is not assent.

This means that vocal resistance is not an obstacle to transformation. It is an information signal — and an extraordinarily valuable one. It identifies what people across the organisation are trying to protect. When we suppress that signal — when we label it resistance, manage it away, route around the "difficult" people — we are not removing an impediment to change. We are destroying our own early warning system. We are disabling Dynamic 4 and guaranteeing that problems will remain invisible until they manifest as the quiet disengagement, the plateauing survey scores, and the talent attrition the original post describes. Sometimes, until catastrophe strikes.

The most dangerous state for any organisation is when voice is suppressed, and exit is not yet exercised. It looks like stability. It looks like the survey scores plateauing. It is, in fact, the organisation's correction mechanisms failing silently.

A Proposition, Not an Accusation

I want to be clear about what this framing is and what it is not.

It is not an accusation that SAP or any specific transformation program here has violated these dynamics through malice or incompetence. Transformation is genuinely hard and the competitive and technological and regulatory environment genuinely demands it. Leaders who launch transformation programs are generally acting in what they believe to be the organisation's best interest, and often they are right about the destination even when the journey causes harm and that harm is genuinely regretted.

But the dynamics operate regardless of intent. A boundary crossed with the best of intentions is still crossed. A value exchange that defaults despite genuine effort still defaults. Good people designing good programs can still produce the patterns the original post describes, if the structural conditions of sustainable exchange are not attended to. And the data is unambiguous: across industries, across geographies, across decades of research, we are systematically overloading organisations beyond their change capacity while the workforce's willingness to absorb that change collapses. This is not a trend that better communications frameworks will reverse. It requires an honest reckoning with the terms of the exchange itself.

What the four-dynamics framework offers is a diagnostic lens. Before asking "how do we communicate this transformation better?" or "how do we pace it more humanely?" — both valid questions — we might ask:

- Boundaries: Have the people affected by this change genuinely consented to the altered terms of their working life, or have we announced and hoped for adjustment?
- Value exchange: Are we delivering measurable value to employees in each phase, or are we asking them to accept present costs against future promises? If the latter, what is our track record of delivery, and why should they extend us credit? Would we extend credit to a customer with the same history?
- Context: Have we provided a coherent, honest picture of what is happening and why — including what we don't know and what isn't working — or are we managing narrative? Are we listening to the information signals our people are sending through their behaviour, their workarounds, and their resistance?
- Voice and exit: Can an employee say "this is not working" without career risk? Can they leave without stigma? If not, we have disabled our own correction mechanisms and should not be surprised when problems go undetected until they are severe. And when someone does speak up, do we treat it as a problem to manage or as a signal that 100 to 200 others are thinking the same thing?

These can be uncomfortable questions. They are also, I would suggest, more precise than asking "are our people fatigued?" — because (1) we already know they are, and (2) they point to specific, actionable causes rather than a generalised state.

A Call to Action

The original post invites us to share what our organisations have done to manage transformation fatigue effectively. I want to extend that invitation in a slightly different direction.

Consider the four dynamics as a diagnostic tool for your own transformation context. Not as theory, but as a practical checklist:

- Where are boundaries being crossed without genuine consent? Not announced consultation, but real negotiation of altered terms.
- Where has the value exchange become unsatisfactory — in volume, in horizon, or both? Where are we asking people to trade real present costs for uncertain future benefits, and what is our credibility position given past delivery?
- Where is context insufficient or contradictory? Where are people expected to perform in conditions they cannot coherently understand? Where are people building shadow systems, and what are those systems telling us about what the official system fails to provide?
- Where is voice unsafe or exit penalised? Where have we inadvertently disabled the mechanisms by which problems get surfaced and corrected? When someone resists, do we ask what they are trying to protect?

If you can answer these questions honestly for your current program, you will have a more actionable diagnosis than any engagement survey can provide.

The further invitation: share what you find. Not just what your organisation did to manage fatigue, but what you discovered when you looked at the underlying exchange. Where were the dynamics being maintained, and what made that possible? Where were they being violated, and what were the real consequences — not the ones in the transformation project steering committee report, but the ones visible at the working level?

The quality of this conversation is itself a test of Dynamic 4. If honest, specific, sometimes uncomfortable responses appear below, it tells us something important about the culture we are both building and living in. For what it is worth, and in response to the OP's invitation, my experience in SAP has been that we are very good at Dynamic 4, compared to most other places I have seen. If the thread fills with carefully worded endorsements — or silence — that tells us something too. Remember Moore's ratio: for every person who speaks, 100 to 200 are thinking the same thing. The comments that appear here, and the ones that don't, are both data.

The original post concludes that "change capacity is a finite, renewable resource to be stewarded with discipline." I agree, and I want to add that it renews when the conditions of fair exchange are restored. It depletes when they are violated. The four dynamics describe those conditions. Whether we attend to them or not, they are still operating. The data — 88% failure rates, 95% for digital, willingness to support change nearly halved in six years, ten annual initiatives against a sustainable capacity of two — tells us that, across the industry, we are not attending to them.

That is not a reason for despair. It is a reason for precision. The dynamics are not mysterious. They are identifiable, diagnosable, and addressable. But they require us to look at transformation not as something we do to an organisation, but as something we negotiate with the people who constitute it. Because in the end, we are not the recipients of transformation. We are the transformation. And the terms on which we participate will determine whether it succeeds or joins the 88%.

NOTE: The framework described here draws on the concept of Connection Dynamics, which synthesises research from psychological contract theory, self-determination theory, organisational justice, subjective value economics, sensemaking theory, information economics, network engineering, and the exit-voice-loyalty tradition into an integrated model of sustainable human exchange. The term "dynamics" is chosen deliberately — these are probabilistic patterns in complex human systems, not mechanical certainties. People differ in both our internals and circumstances, which is precisely why value is a matter of subjective judgment rather than material measurement. You cannot measure it with a ruler. But the patterns, when violated, are remarkably consistent in their consequences.

entry.title: Custom CDS Views / Analytical Queries Not Visible in SAC Live Connection via SAP_COM_0065
entry.content:
I successfully set up a Live Data Connection between SAC and S/4HANA Public Cloud using OAuth authentication via communication scenario SAP_COM_0065. The connection is validated and working — when browsing sources in SAC, I can see approximately 100 standard SAP queries (e.g. 2CCENGPTEBILLITMQ and similar standard delivered queries).

However, my custom developments are completely invisible in SAC. Specifically:

  • Custom CDS views of type Analytical Cube that I created in the Custom CDS Views app do not appear as available sources in SAC.
  • Custom Analytical Queries that I created on top of both my custom cubes and SAP-delivered released cubes (published with green status) are also not showing up in SAC.
  • Only SAP standard delivered queries that are pre-registered to SAP_COM_0065 are visible.

entry.title: Re: Restrict revoking of TECO in CO02 for some users in S...
entry.content:
Thank you for the detailed explanation and references.

After reviewing available authorization objects and restrictions for CO02, if I've understood correctly, it's technically impossible in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition to achieve user-specific granular control where:

User A (restricted): Can access CO02, change non-TECO orders, but cannot do Functions > Revoke TECO on TECO orders

User B (full): Can do everything including revoke TECO

Core issue: Change Production Order catalogs bundle field changes + status actions (BUTA/UNTECO) together, with no ACTVT=61-specific or B_USERSTAT-like restriction field available in Public Cloud business roles - unlike on-premise flexibility.

entry.title: EBS - Processing Rules overlap | SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Good morning,

we are trying to set up EBS automatic Processing Rules in SAP S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition system. Specifically, we are trying to create some "generic" rules base on the following conditions: company code, external transaction, house bank and house bank account.

However, we would like to add some more "specific" rules: so, for the same company code, external transaction, house bank and house bank account, we are considering to add the "contains" condition to the Memo Line attribute. This "specifc" rule will write in a different G/L.

Unfortunately, there will be an overlap between the "generic" rule and both of them may be satisfied: so, the system will not apply any of those rules (SAP note 3623221).

It seems that the only option is using "does not contain" condition for Memo Line attribute in the "generic" rule, but this will be diffcult to be managed when the number of keyowords/"specific" rules increases.

Are there any options to manage this situation?

Thank you

entry.title: Re: Post goods receipt referenced to "pending truck cargo...
entry.content:
Dear @GuillerminaPdelC,

In Public Cloud there is no standard interim movement before 101 that would make the stock immediately available for sale and only later validate it against the supplier electronic cargo bill. If the stock must be available on the truck right away the standard approach is to post the actual GR with movement 101 based on the confirmed physical receipt, if prior verification is required first the supported standard is a 2-step GR (103/105 or 107/109) but then the stock is blocked and cannot be used for sales immediately

Summary:

The requirement like you wrote makes business sense but SAP standard does not provide a separate pre-101, sell-now, verify-later movement type in Public Cloud. The closest standard fit is either 101 immediately or 2-step blocked-stock receipt

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Resource Handover for Product WTs in S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition
entry.content:
Dear Experts,

 

I would like to ask for your help and technical guidance on how to manage a resource handover within a single 2-step Product Warehouse Task (Replenishment) in S/4HANA Cloud, public edition.

Process Facts:

  • Context: EWM Stock Replenishment between two buildings.
  • Execution: Due to the physical distance, the process is split between two employees. Resource A performs the source confirmation (picking) in Building 1. Resource B performs the destination confirmation (putaway) in Building 2.
  • System Environment: Product-based WTs (no HUs used). Layout-Oriented Storage Control (LOSC) is not implemented.
  • Current Issue: After Resource A confirms the source data, the Warehouse Order remains assigned to Resource A. When Resource B attempts to confirm the destination, the system prevents it (Error /SCWM/RF_EN140).

Questions:

  1. What is the standard procedure to release the resource assignment after the first step (source confirmation) so a second resource can take over and confirm the destination? (I have even tried in Warehouse Monitor to unassign the old resource and then assign a new one, but it still says that the old res. is processing the task)
  2. Is there a standard Exception Code available in Public Cloud to trigger this handover for Product WTs?
  3. How can the "Stock on Resource" be transferred from Resource A to Resource B within the same Warehouse Task?

Best regards,

Adam Toth

entry.title: Re: Released CDS View for Sales Price Condition Records w...
entry.content:
Dear @RudhraU,

Question 1.
Yes but not in I_SlsPrcgConditionRecord. SAP explicitly says that I_SlsPrcgConditionRecord does not contain Material as designed and recommends using API_SLSPRICINGCONDITIONRECORD_SRV instead. SAP also states that for product/price overview you should use A_SlsPrcgCndnRecdValidity, fetched through the OData service

More details below:

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3332533

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3539477

https://userapps.support.sap.com/sap/support/knowledge/en/3521811

Question 2.
Based on SAP roadmap I could not identify any public commitment for a separate new released pricing CDS with Material. Based on the official SAP Notes (above) current supported direction is to use API_SLSPRICINGCONDITIONRECORD_SRV / A_SlsPrcgCndnRecdValidity, not I_SlsPrcgConditionRecord for this requirement

More details below and SAP Notes above:

https://roadmaps.sap.com/board?range=CURRENT-LAST&PRODUCT=67837800100800007389#Q1%202026

Question 3.
SAP provides the required data through two different released artifacts I_MaterialStock_2 for stock data and API_SLSPRICINGCONDITIONRECORD_SRV for sales pricing data. Since the pricing data is exposed via a released remote OData API rather than a released I_* CDS view intended for direct CDS-based reuse the SAP-aligned clean-core approach is to compose both datasets in an extensibility/integration layer and expose the merged result as the external API

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/c0c54048d35849128be8e872df5bea6d/0bb40d9e5cd14a6c8c556bacc8056e02.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/2a17a3e1525f4f46a9c5156bd2d3bc87.html

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/0f2be3d26b7f48b0b0c426c188b55a89.html

Question 4.
Yes. SAP extensibility guidance says side-by-side extensions on SAP BTP are the standard pattern for decoupled applications and hub scenarios and that on-stack ABAP Cloud can also be used to create custom remote APIs when needed. SAP also documents that custom CDS views can be exposed as external OData APIs in Public Cloud so for a single external-facing Stock + Price API a composition layer in SAP BTP is the cleanest recommendation an on-stack option would need to be validated against the actually released objects and integration capabilities available in your tenant

More details below:

https://help.sap.com/docs/SAP_S4HANA_CLOUD/0f69f8fb28ac4bf48d2b57b9637e81fa/f4dcfd6e14b54177b98d6099ddb2014e.html

Best Regards,

Dawid

entry.title: Re: Query on automatic distribution of license management...
entry.content:
Automatic maintenance certificate distribution will try to renew certificates about 30 days before expiration.

Is there any specific reason you would like to delete a maintenance certificate? I will not suggest to delete the maintenance certificate.

Only ABAP and JAVA type systems are relevant for maintenance certificate. Database systems are not relevant for this function. Listed systems are systems available in LMDB. If a desired system is not in the list, please manually add it to LMDB first.

entry.title: Re: govern Cost Center authorizations in GI & PR
entry.content:
Hi @Amani_Mirdhah ,
Yes, SAP can govern Cost Center usage in both Goods Issue (GI) and Purchase Requisitions (PR) through authorization objects, but it requires using the correct SAP authorization objects (mainly K_CCA and K_CCA_USR) and sometimes workflow or custom enhancements.

If you want strict blocking then use K_CCA_USR + BAdI ME_PROCESS_REQ_CUST to enforce cost center authorization at PR creation and GI posting.

If you want governance via approval then use workflow to route PRs to cost center owners.

If you want minimal configuration then you can assign cost centers to users in K_CCA_USR and rely on standard checks.

entry.title: Re: Unable to Remove MIGO from Movement Type 341 in OMJJ...
entry.content:
Hi @Ash_22 ,
You added an entry in OMJJ for movement type 341, but SAP considers that table entry part of SAP‑delivered configuration, so once it exists in the system, SAP’s protection logic prevents you from deleting it manually. That’s why you now get the message “Do not make any changes (SAP entry)”. The system is treating your manually created entry as if it were SAP‑standard.

My suggestion to fix this issue as mentioned below.
If your client is set to “No changes allowed,” OMJJ will block deletions.
Go to SCC4
Select your client (100)
Check:
Changes to Repository and Cross‑Client Customizing Allowed
Cross‑client object changes
Save
Retry deleting the entry in OMJJ

Hope the above solution work with you.

entry.title: Re: How to authorize Direct Debit Payments outside SEPA in Contract Accounting
entry.content: Hello, we had to remove the reference for FI-AR as currently no plan exists in this direction. If the functionality is demanded for FI-AR, please submit the request via  the SAP Customer Influence portal is at: **https://influence.sap.com** You'll need your SAP Universal ID (S-User) to login. Before submitting a new request, search for existing ones and vote on them if they match your needs - this is often more effective as SAP prioritizes requests with more customer votes.

entry.title: Re: Week 2 : Use Joule Chat to create ABAP RAP Application and explore the Explain feature
entry.content:
Challenge completed.

I went with an application to track game collection (since it is close to my interests).
It is semi fictional whereby the game gains resale value as of 7 years old, in reality this should be way higher as it does not really apply for latest or previous gen games and even then the game itself plays a role as well :D.

entry.title: Re: Status of the study program with reassignment in LMS
entry.content: @MaoAcevedo, @jackoneill23 stands to be correct. That highlighted curricula (programa de estudio) doesn’t seem to have retraining set. Take a look at the following documentation for additional details: Retraining in SAP SuccessFactors Learning Curricula.

entry.title: Re: S/4HANA Public Cloud: How to restrict Credit Release...
entry.content: Hi Chris, thank you for the information provided. As we need to set this up, we were looking for the App "Manage Credit Management Rules" to maintain the relevant information. Unfortunately, we cannot find the App - not in the App Finder, not in the Customer System, not in the Partner Demo System. We wanted to check the related Scope Items, but there is no information in the Fiori Apps Library: https://fioriappslibrary.hana.ondemand.com/sap/fix/externalViewer/#/detail/Apps('UKM_BRF_CONFIG(UKM_BRF_CONFIG_AC)')/S36 Could you please help us out? Thanks and best regards, Katja

entry.title: Re: Service instances stuck in "Deletion in Progress" on...
entry.content:
Hello,

This is expected behavior. Service instances for plans "hdi-shared" & "schema" can be only deleted if the underlying database is accessible. 

The only way to remove this "orphan" service instances is the purge-service-instance command, please try to find somebody within your organization with corresponding access rights to execute:

cf purge-service-instance <HANA_Cloud_Instance_ServiceID>

If no one can run this command, then you may need to open a case with SAP Technical Support under component BC-XS-SRV-HSB.

Hope it helps.

Best Regards,

Hugo
